K–2.title: Debugging with Story Sequences
K–2.objective: Students will identify and correct errors in a simple story sequence.
K–2.task: Students work with mixed-up picture cards from a familiar story, place them in the correct order, and explain how they fixed the mistake.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens as students explain why a card belongs first, next, or last.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a corrected 4-step story sequence and retell it to a partner.
K–2.udl: Provide visual picture cards, sentence frames, and verbal retell options.
K–2.materials: Story cards, pocket chart, crayons, glue, sequencing mat.
K–2.vocabulary: debug, sequence, first, next, last, fix
K–2.procedures:
1. Introduce debugging as fixing mistakes.
2. Model correcting a mixed-up story.
3. Students work in pairs to fix a story sequence.
4. Students share their corrected sequence.
5. Reflect on how they found the mistake.
K–2.differentiation: Use 3-step sequences for support. Offer challenge cards with extra distractors for advanced learners.
K–2.reflection: How did you know the story was out of order?

3–5.title: Pattern Recognition with Emoji Sorting
3–5.objective: Students will identify patterns and sorting rules using emoji cards.
3–5.task: Students sort emoji cards into categories they define, explain the rule, and compare their categories with a partner.
3–5.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether students can clearly explain the pattern or rule they used.
3–5.summativeAssessment: Students create a final sorting chart and write one sentence explaining the rule.
3–5.udl: Provide printed emoji cards, color-coded categories, and optional verbal explanation.
3–5.materials: Emoji cards, chart paper, markers, sorting mats.
3–5.procedures:
1. Introduce pattern recognition.
2. Model sorting a small emoji set.
3. Students sort emoji cards.
4. Students compare rules with a partner.
5. Students complete a final chart.
3–5.differentiation: Provide smaller card sets for support. Challenge advanced learners to sort by two attributes.
3–5.reflection: What rule did you use, and how did it help organize the emojis?

6–8.title: Intro to Variables with Scratch
6–8.objective: Students will use variables in Scratch to store and display changing information.
6–8.task: Students build a Scratch mini-project that asks for user input, stores it in a variable, and displays the result on screen.
6–8.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether students can identify what the variable stores and when it changes.
6–8.summativeAssessment: Students submit a working Scratch project using at least one variable and a short explanation of its purpose.
6–8.udl: Provide a starter Scratch file, visual coding reference sheet, and partner support.
6–8.materials: Computers, Scratch accounts, coding guide, projector.
6–8.procedures:
1. Introduce variables with real-world examples.
2. Model creating and changing a variable in Scratch.
3. Students build a guided mini-project.
4. Students test and revise projects.
5. Students share completed projects.
6–8.differentiation: Provide a starter template for support. Challenge advanced learners to use multiple variables.
6–8.reflection: How did the variable help your program work?

9–12.title: Data Visualizations with Spreadsheets
9–12.objective: Students will analyze a small data set and create an appropriate chart in a spreadsheet application.
9–12.task: Students import a data table into a spreadsheet, calculate one summary insight, and build a chart that communicates a clear conclusion.
9–12.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether students selected a chart type that matches the data and can explain why.
9–12.summativeAssessment: Students submit a completed spreadsheet with chart and a short written analysis.
9–12.udl: Provide a sample data set, step-by-step guide, and optional video directions.
9–12.materials: Computers, spreadsheet software, sample data set, guided notes.
9–12.vocabulary: data set, chart, visualization, analysis, trend
9–12.procedures:
1. Introduce data visualization principles.
2. Model importing data and creating a chart.
3. Students analyze a provided data set.
4. Students create a chart and written analysis.
5. Class discussion on chart choices.
9–12.differentiation: Provide a partially completed spreadsheet for support. Challenge advanced learners to compare two chart types.
9–12.reflection: Why was your chart the best choice for this data?
